text: Financial Institutions & Wealth Managers Savings Tips Money Talks Legal & Financial Planning Fraud & Scams Financial Caregiving 101 Alzheimer’s & Dementia Alzheimer’s & Dementia Memory, Judgment & Money: Why Brain Changes Make Scams Easier By Jenny Leight • January 21, 2026 Copy Link URL Copied! We like to think we’d never fall for a scam. But when the brain starts to change, even the sharpest people can become vulnerable. Suddenly, that “urgent” call sounds convincing, and that online deal looks like a great opportunity. Memory, judgment, and emotions protect us in countless ways. Understanding how they change with age helps families stay one step ahead of scammers who try to take advantage. The Brain and the Bank Account Are More Connected Than You Think Scientists have found that certain parts of the brain that help us manage money, like judgment, attention, and emotional regulation, are the same areas affected early in cognitive decline. That means small cognitive changes can potentially show up first in our finances. Think about what it takes to handle money well: paying attention to details, remembering passwords, double-checking statements, spotting too-good-to-be-true offers. As those mental muscles weaken, a person may start to overlook red flags or struggle to make sound financial choices, even if everything else still seems “normal.” How Subtle Cognitive Changes Open the Door for Scammers Not all brain changes look the same. Here’s how a few common ones can make someone more vulnerable: Memory loss: Forgetting past conversations or payments makes it easier for scammers to repeat their pitch or collect payment more than once, without the person realizing it’s the same scam. Poor judgment: Offers that once seemed suspicious now sound convincing. A promise of guaranteed returns or “urgent tech help” feels worth the risk. Slower processing speed: Scammers know how to create pressure. When someone needs extra time to think but feels rushed, they’re more likely to comply. Reduced attention: Missing small clues like a misspelled name or a strange email address can make fake messages look legitimate. Emotional changes: Loneliness or grief can make people more likely to trust strangers who show kindness and give attention. Each of these changes can affect how someone interacts with money, technology, and people, and that’s exactly how scammers take advantage of these vulnerabilities. How to Protect Your Loved One (and Their Independence) You don’t have to take over someone’s finances to help
